data.title: Most shorted small cap stocks as of mid-April led by healthcare sector
data.description:
Short sellers are concentrating their bets heavily in the small-cap space as of mid-April, with healthcare, and biotechnology in particular, drawing the most attention. The following list ranks the 10 most heavily shorted small-cap stocks by short interest as a percentage of float.

The list is topped by KalVista Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (KALV), with a short interest of 39.75% of float. Recursion Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (RXRX) and Intellia Therapeutics, Inc. (NTLA) follow closely behind, with Groupon, Inc. (GRPN) and indie Semiconductor, Inc. (INDI) rounding out the top five.

The list features a mix of Quant Ratings, from Buy ratings for KalVista Pharmaceuticals (KALV) and NETSTREIT Corp. (NTST) to Strong Sell ratings for Groupon (GRPN), indie Semiconductor (INDI), EVgo, Inc. (EVGO), and C3.ai, Inc. (AI). Biotechnology companies are well represented on the list, with KALV, RXRX, NTLA, and SELLAS Life Sciences Group, Inc. (SLS) all operating in that sector.

Here is the list:

  1. KalVista Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (KALV), Short interest: 39.75%

  2. Recursion Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (RXRX), Short interest: 33.32%

  3. Intellia Therapeutics, Inc. (NTLA), Short interest: 32.97%

  4. Groupon, Inc. (GRPN), Short interest: 32.62%

  5. indie Semiconductor, Inc. (INDI), Short interest: 32.58%

  6. EVgo, Inc. (EVGO), Short interest: 30.91%

  7. Beyond Meat, Inc. (BYND), Short interest: 30.57%

  8. SELLAS Life Sciences Group, Inc. (SLS), Short interest: 29.81%

  9. C3.ai, Inc. (AI), Short interest: 29.79%

  10. NETSTREIT Corp. (NTST), Short interest: 29.50%

Small-Cap ETFs: (IJR), (IWM), (VB), (VBR), (VBK), (SCHA), (SPSM), and (DFAS)

data.title: Catalyst Watch: Tesla talks AI5 chips, Intel reports, and the Warner Bros. deal goes to a vote
data.description:
Welcome to Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch - a breakdown of some of next week's actionable events that stand out. Read more about the events that may impact stock prices next week.

 

Monday - April 20

  • Volatility watch - Car rental stocks Avis Budget (CAR) and Hertz Global (HTZ) are set up for another volatile week of trading based on options volume. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Mega Fortune (MGRT), Avanos Medical (AVNS), and Willow Lane Acquisition (WLAC). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include PepGen (PEPG), MiMedx (MDXG), and WW International (WW). Short interest is elevated on Lucid Group (LCID) and C3.ai (AI) again.
  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Steel Dynamics (STLD) and Wintrust Financial (WTFC).
  • Dividend watch - Companies that have an ex-dividend date coming next week include Caterpillar (CAT), Dell Technologies (DELL), Lowe's Companies (LOW), and CVS Health (CVS).
  • IPO watch - Convenience store chain Yesway (YSWY), healthcare REIT National Healthcare Properties (HLTC), and Elmet Group (ELMT) are expected to price their IPOs and begin trading. The IPO lockup periods expire on blocks of shares of Charming Medical (MCTA) and Bgin Blockchain (BGIN). The quiet period ends on Ursb (URSB) to free up analysts to post ratings.
  • Iran watch - U.S. and Iranian officials are expected to meet for talks next week in Pakistan. The ceasefire is set to expire on April 21.
  • All day - Tempus AI (TEM) will present research at the AACR annual meeting.
  • All day - The three-day Adobe Summit will begin. Adobe (ADBE) is expected to unveil major product and strategy updates. Analysts think the event will serve as a window into how the company is positioning its platform against broader AI adoption trends.

 

Tuesday - April 21

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include GE Aerospace (GE), UnitedHealth Group (UNH), RTX (RTX), Northrop Grumman (NOC), 3M Company (MMM), United Airlines (UAL), and Capital One (COF). Options trading implies a double-digit swing for Equifax (EFX) after it reports.
  • All day - WTI crude oil May futures expire amid a very volatile period for the oil market.
  • All day - The Senate Banking Committee will hold a confirmation hearing for Kevin Warsh and his appointment to be the Federal Reserve chairman.
  • 7:30 a.m. GE Aerospace (GE) will hold its earnings call. Updates on GE's commercial engines/services growth, engine deliveries, supply chain execution, margin outlook, and guidance are anticipated. Options trading implies a 7% share price move after the release. Howmet Aerospace (HWM) is the stock with the tightest trading correlation to GE on earnings day. 
  • 8:30 a.m. The March Retail Sales report will be released by the U.S. Census Bureau.

 

Wednesday - April 22

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Tesla (TSLA), Lam Research (LRCX), Philip Morris (PM), IBM (IBM), Texas Instruments (TXN), AT&T (T), Boeing (BA), Moody's (MCO), Las Vegas Sands (LVS), and CSX (CSX). Options trading implies a double-digit swing for ServiceNow (NOW) after it reports.
  • All day - The three-day Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) Cloud Next event in Las Vegas will begin. The spotlight will be on the company's latest work in generative AI, infrastructure, and security, with a strong focus on practical product updates and developer tools.
  • All day - Calix (CALX) will hold its Investor Day event.
  • 4:00 p.m. Tesla (TSLA) will hold its earnings conference call. Analysts expect updates on the robotaxi rollout, Optimus, the AI5 chip timeline, and the company's AI-capex plans. Options trading implies a 6% share price move after the report drops. Stocks that could be impacted by Elon Musk's presentation include Rivian (RIVN), Lucid (LCID), General Motors (GM), Ford (F), and even Nvidia (NVDA) and Taiwan Semi (TSM) based on the AI5 update.

 

Thursday - April 23

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Intel (INTC), American Express (AXP), Union Pacific (UNP), Lockheed Martin (LMT), Comcast (CMCSA), and Baker Hughes (BKR). Options trading implies a double-digit swing for Appfolio (APPF) and Mobileye Global (MBLY) after they report.
  • All day - Shareholders with Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) will vote on the buyout by Paramount (PSKY).
  • 9:10 a.m. Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) will hold its annual meeting. Shareholders will vote on a say-for-pay proposal and an independent board chair proposal.
  • 5:00 p.m. Intel (INTC) will hold its earnings call. Key topics will include updates on supply constraints, margin expectations, and AI-centric computing/data center demand. Options trading implies a 9% share price move after the earnings release.
  • 8:00 p.m. The NFL draft will take place at Acrisure Stadium in Pittsburgh. Betting around the draft has exploded in just the last two years for DraftKings (DKNG), FanDuel (FLUT), and BetMGM (MGM) (GMVHF). This year, prediction markets Kalshi (KALSHI) and Polymarket (POLYMARKET) will be much more involved.

 

Friday - April 24

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Procter & Gamble (PG), and Charter Communications (CHTR).
  • All day - The Beijing International Auto Show will begin. Domestic Chinese brands BYD (BYDDF), NIO (NIO), XPeng (XPEV), and others will unveil new intelligent EVs that push energy density, charging speed, and driver-assistance capabilities. BMW (BAMXF), Mercedes-Benz (MBGAF), and Porsche (POAHY) will also be participating.
  • 10:00 a.m. The release of the University of Michigan survey for April will be closely watched by economists for the update on consumer sentiment.

 

Seeking Alpha editor Josh Fineman contributed to this article.

data.title: There is reasonable probability that the Strait of Hormuz reopens quickly, says Fed's Waller
data.description:
There is a reasonable probability that the Strait of Hormuz reopens quickly, according to Federal Reserve Board Governor Christopher Waller.

Waller was speaking on the economic outlook before the Auburn University Department of Economics David Kaserman Memorial Lecture event.

"This prospect probably represents a best-case scenario for the economy," noted Waller.

"The pass-through of higher energy prices to other goods and services should be limited. And despite the pain caused by higher energy prices, consumers, and businesses will understand that the worst is past, energy prices should begin to recede, and this view will tend to support ongoing growth in spending, production, and hiring," said the Fed governor.

However, the worst-case scenario also has a reasonable probability, according to Waller.

"Securities markets in general seem to be undervaluing the risk that the conflict continues, the Strait remains closed, and disruptions to production and shipping keep energy prices high," said Waller.

"For inflation, the risk is that the longer the conflict drags on and energy prices remain high, the more likely it is that these elevated prices will bleed into other prices, as businesses incorporate costly energy input costs in setting their prices," said Waller.

The Strait of Hormuz is now "completely open" for all commercial vessels for the rest of the ceasefire timeline, Iran said today. President Donald Trump, however, said the naval blockade will remain in effect.

 

Developing... Check back for updates.

data.title: Strait of Hormuz 'effectively closed' even after Iran announcement - report
data.description:
Despite Iran’s announcement earlier Friday that the Strait of Hormuz has reopened, commodities-data agency Kpler said that the critical waterway “remains effectively closed,” according to a media report.

Most shipping organizations continue to classify the strait as too unsafe for transit due to the presence of mines, the Wall Street Journal reported.

Iran’s military has also stated it would block any vessel it doesn’t authorize, casting further doubt on the viability of resumed shipping operations.

Even if Tehran permits a genuine reopening, Kpler estimated that only about 25% of Middle East Gulf export capacity would resume within one to two months, the WSJ reported.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Affiliated Managers ticks lower on short call from Orso Partners
data.description:
Affiliated Managers Group (AMG) edged lower by 1.5% after a short call on the company was disclosed.

Hedge fund Orso Partners has taken a short position in Affiliated Managers, according to a Bloomberg report, which cites a letter the fund sent to investors.

The short call against AMG, the investment firm that's backed by Cliff Asness's AQR Capital Management, is based on concerns about tax loss harvesting strategies that may be curtailed by regulators. 

AQR’s recent growth “is built on regulatory arbitrage that faces immediate scrutiny,” Orso portfolio manager Nathan Koppikar said in a letter to investors. “The most direct way to play the inevitable fallout” is by wagering against Affiliated Managers, he said.

Affiliated Managers (AMG) didn't immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

data.title: Regions projects 2026 net interest income growth of 2.5%-4% with net interest margin exiting in the low 3.70%s
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Regions Financial Corporation (RF) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “This morning, we reported strong first quarter earnings of $539 million or $0.62 per share,” said (President, CEO & Chairman John Turner), adding, “We grew loans and deposits on both an average and ending basis, and our credit metrics continue to improve as we resolve our portfolios of interest.”

  • On customers and demand, (President, CEO & Chairman Turner) said, “Conversations with customers suggest that despite recent volatility, sentiment remains generally optimistic,” while also noting, “We are seeing some pressure among lower income customers, but larger income tax refunds compared to last year have helped offset a portion of that impact.”

  • Regions highlighted core tech milestones and timeline, with (President, CEO & Chairman Turner) saying, “We’re on track to deploy our commercial lending system and small business digital origination platform this summer,” and adding, “We expect to launch a pilot in the third quarter and begin conversion in 2027.”

  • Management change was a focal point, with (President, CEO & Chairman Turner) stating, “After a nearly 40-year career at Regions, she’s made the decision to retire,” referring to Dana Nolan.

  • Balance sheet mix and loan quality were emphasized by (Senior Executive VP & CFO Anil Chadha): “Ending loans grew 2%, while average loans increased approximately 1%,” and “Almost 2/3 of the growth was investment-grade credits with the majority of the remaining growth near investment grade.”

Outlook

  • Net interest income guidance was reiterated by (Senior Executive VP & CFO Chadha): “For full year 2026, we reiterate our net interest income expectation of between 2.5% and 4% growth and for the net interest margin to exit the year in the low 3.70%s.”

  • Near-term trajectory was framed explicitly by (Senior Executive VP & CFO Chadha): “In the second quarter, we expect a strong rebound with approximately 2% net interest income growth, followed by additional expansion in subsequent quarters.”

  • Fee outlook was reiterated by (Senior Executive VP & CFO Chadha): “For full year 2026, we continue to expect adjusted noninterest income to grow between 3% and 5% versus 2025,” while noting capital markets should be “trending near the lower end of the range in the second quarter and moving higher thereafter” within the “$90 million to $105 million range.”

  • Versus the prior quarter’s messaging, Regions maintained the same full-year NII growth range and the same adjusted noninterest income growth range, while current-quarter prepared remarks added the securities repositioning and a specific expectation for Q2 NII growth.

Financial Results

  • Profitability and returns were led by (President, CEO & Chairman Turner): “We reported strong first quarter earnings of $539 million or $0.62 per share,” and “we generated a return on tangible common equity of 18%.”

  • Net interest income and margin commentary centered on mix and spreads, with (Senior Executive VP & CFO Chadha) stating, “As expected, net interest income was lower linked quarter,” and “margin came in below expectations for the quarter, reflecting tighter asset spreads as a result of market conditions, paydowns of higher-yielding loans and remixing into higher quality credits.”

  • Regions disclosed a post-quarter balance sheet action: (Senior Executive VP & CFO Chadha) said, “Subsequent to quarter end, higher interest rates created an opportunity to sell approximately $900 million of shorter duration securities… at a $40 million loss,” and described it as “carrying a short approximately 2-year payback period.”

  • Credit metrics improved, with (Senior Executive VP & CFO Chadha) reporting: “Annualized net charge-offs… decreased… to 54 basis points,” “the NPL ratio declined… to 71 basis points,” and “the allowance for credit losses declined $39 million.”

  • Capital actions and ratios were detailed by (Senior Executive VP & CFO Chadha): “We ended the quarter with an estimated common equity Tier 1 ratio of 10.7%, while executing $401 million in share repurchases and paying $227 million in common dividends.”

Q&A

  • Ryan Nash, Goldman Sachs: Asked about confidence within NII and fee ranges; (Senior Executive VP & CFO Chadha) responded, “we’re very confident in hitting the ranges,” citing “$2.3 billion point-to-point” loan growth, and said deposit costs exited at “1.69%.”

  • Robert Siefers, Piper Sandler: Pressed on margin drivers; (Senior Executive VP & CFO Chadha) answered, “managing deposit costs is still the primary mechanism,” and added fixed asset repricing opportunities of “about $9 billion looking forward.”

  • John Pancari, Evercore: Asked about Southeast deposit competition and asset-spread compression; (Senior Executive VP & CFO Chadha) said the backdrop has been “highly competitive… for north of a year,” and added tighter spreads are “primarily in larger C&I.”

  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: Asked whether line draws were defensive and what lifts capital markets revenue; (Senior Executive VP & CFO Chadha) said, “I wouldn’t call it defensive,” while (President, CEO & Chairman Turner) tied upside to “real estate capital markets” improving “as rates… come down.”

  • Gerard Cassidy, RBC: Asked about macro uncertainty in reserves and NDFI strategy; (Senior Executive VP & CFO Chadha) pointed to “about $17 million… tied to macro uncertainty,” while (President, CEO & Chairman Turner) said Regions is “more cautious” and wants private-credit relationships that can “gain deposits” and “participate in capital markets activity.”

  • David Chiaverini, Jefferies: Asked about AI-enabled cash optimization and hiring amid footprint M&A; (Senior Executive VP & CFO Chadha) said Regions customers’ “average deposits is about $5,200,” and (President, CEO & Chairman Turner) said the bank has completed “more than 2/3 of the hiring” in its plan.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative to neutral analyst tone, with questions repeatedly probing softer NIM versus expectations, competitive deposit pricing, the durability of line draws, and sensitivity to proposed capital rules.

  • Slightly positive management tone, with (Senior Executive VP & CFO Chadha) stating, “we’re very confident in hitting the ranges,” and framing multiple tailwinds (loan growth, deposit costs, fixed-rate turnover) while declining to “get too far ahead of the proposed rule.”

  • Versus the prior quarter, the current call carried similar confidence on full-year ranges but faced more detailed questioning on spread compression, the quality/nature of utilization-driven growth, and how Basel proposals might change capital flexibility.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Leadership changes shifted from CFO transition to IR transition: Q4 introduced “our newly appointed CFO” (Anil Chadha), while Q1 focused on Dana Nolan’s retirement.

  • Credit discussion stayed constructive, but Q1 emphasized specific normalization paths, with (President, CEO & Chairman Turner) saying the bank is in the “latter innings” on transportation and expects NPAs not to move “too much further than 71 basis points.”

  • Balance sheet narrative moved from Q4’s “loan growth was challenged in 2025” and runoff to Q1’s “broad-based C&I lending” and stronger late-quarter momentum.

  • Capital framework attention increased, with Q1 providing a pro forma fully implemented Basel III CET1 view and repeated caution about timing and constituents beyond regulators.

Risks and Concerns

  • Market volatility and spread pressure were central risks, with (Senior Executive VP & CFO Chadha) citing “tighter asset spreads,” “paydowns of higher-yielding loans,” and “remixing into higher quality credits.”

  • Regulatory uncertainty was flagged by (Senior Executive VP & CFO Chadha): “The timing… the phase-in schedule… will matter a lot,” and he said the bank will not “get too far ahead of the proposed rule.”

  • Credit uncertainty remains tied to specific work-out timing, with (President, CEO & Chairman Turner) stating, “the timing of which we get back within that range is still not entirely clear,” referring to the charge-off outlook.

Final Takeaway

Regions management emphasized a Q1 marked by higher earnings, improving credit metrics, and renewed loan momentum, while reiterating full-year 2026 targets for net interest income growth and fee growth. Executives also highlighted a post-quarter securities repositioning, ongoing deposit-cost discipline in a competitive environment, and concrete modernization milestones (commercial lending and small-business origination this summer; core deposit pilot in Q3; conversion beginning in 2027), alongside caution on the timing and external interpretation of proposed Basel capital changes.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: S&P 500’s bottom to record levels marks fastest recovery in 36 years
data.description:
The S&P 500’s (SP500) record-breaking recovery from near-correction territory to all-time highs is “completely warranted,” according to Phil Camporeale, multi-asset portfolio manager at JPMorgan Asset Management.

In an interview with CNBC, Camporeale noted that the index’s move from a 9% bottom to record levels in just eleven trading days marks the fastest such recovery in 36 years, driven by improving sentiment around oil prices (CL1:COM), (CO1:COM) and stabilizing volatility measures.

Camporeale pointed to the normalization of both interest rate and equity market volatility as key indicators supporting continued risk-taking.

The MOVE index (or Merrill Lynch Option Volatility Estimate), which measures interest rate volatility, and the VIX (VIX) have returned to late February levels.

“Interest rate volatility, I think, promotes taking risk as long as it stays contained,” Camporeale said, adding that JPMorgan maintained its U.S. equity overweight position throughout the recent turbulence because they believed the domestic market was insulated.

The portfolio manager outlined strong fundamental drivers behind the rally, including double-digit earnings forecasts for the S&P 500 (SP500) and an above-trend U.S. GDP forecast of approximately 2.1% to 2.2%.

Addressing concerns about elevated energy prices potentially curbing market enthusiasm, Camporeale noted that JPMorgan’s analysis shows U.S. GDP would remain around 2.1% even if oil averages $88 per barrel for the entire year.

He acknowledged there are “winners and losers” from higher energy costs, with the U.S. (SP500), (COMP:IND), (DJI) remaining JPMorgan’s preferred investment region, followed by emerging markets (EEM), while Europe (VGK), (IEV) faces greater vulnerability due to their energy dependence.

Camporeale also highlighted current fiscal policy as a buffer against rising consumer costs. He referenced the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, noting that while higher gas prices will “hurt a lot of people,” the timing of fiscal support provides an important offset.

“We have this plus of fiscal policy this year that is happening at a really good time,” he said, emphasizing that this cushion helps prevent the economic outlook from becoming overly pessimistic despite energy-related headwinds.

data.title: Belgian pharma UCB to buy U.S.-based Neurona for up to $1.15B
data.description:
Belgian pharma company UCB (UCBJY) on Friday announced an agreement to acquire Neurona Therapeutics, a U.S.-based developer of cell therapies for neuronal diseases, for a total consideration of up to $1.15.

Following the acquisition, UCB (UCBJF) will add Neurona’s regenerative cell therapies targeting nervous system disorders, including lead asset NRTX-1001, which is designed to treat temporal lobe epilepsy, the most common form of focal epilepsy.

The Brussels-based company is required to pay $650M upfront in addition to potential milestone payments worth up to $500M as part of the agreement, which is expected to close by the end of Q2 2026.

Despite the transaction, UCB (UCBJY) disclosed that its outlook for 2026 revenue will remain unchanged at a high single-digit to low double-digit percentage range of growth.

However, the company revised its guidance for 2026 adjusted EBITDA to indicate high single-digit to mid-teens percentage range growth on a currency-adjusted basis, compared to the prior forecast of a high single-digit to high teens percentage range.

data.title: Bitcoin escapes $75K cage, taps $78K—here's what bulls see next
data.description:
Bitcoin (BTC-USD) has finally broken its 2-month cage range of ~$64K-$75K and jumped ~5% in just 24 hours, reaching the ~$78K level. The overall pattern since February shows a typical cycle: sharp selloff → sideways movement → higher lows → finally breakout. 

Looking deeper, in February, Bitcoin (BTC-USD) dropped hard to ~$63K with heavy selling. In March, it moved sideways between the ~$65K-72K range, showing accumulation.

By early April, price started making higher lows while staying around ~$75K resistance. However, the current breakout confirms that buyers have gained control, pushing the asset towards a bullish phase.

Technical indicators from the TradingView price chart have also supported this move. RSI is around ~73, showing strong momentum; MACD is still bullish, and volume has increased during the breakout.

Reasons behind this rally:

  1. Positive news around the PARITY Act reportedly suggests stablecoin transactions in the U.S. could become tax-free, which is good for cryptocurrency market growth.
  2. The altcoin market added ~$10B and reached ~$190B. Coins like Ethereum (ETH-USD), XRP (XRP-USD), BNB (BNB-USD), and Solana (SOL-USD) also moved up around 3%-5%, helping overall sentiment.
  3. Global tension eased after a recent Truth Social post by Donald J. Trump, where he signaled stability in the Strait of Hormuz and hinted that the war is over, boosting investor confidence.
  4. According to the SoSovalue weekly data from April 13-17, ETF flows have also turned positive. Starting from ~$300M outflows to flipping to ~$400M inflows and then staying positive shows that buyers interest has surged. 
  5. Major bulls like MicroStrategy (MSTR), BlackRock (BLK), and Goldman Sachs (GS) continue to support Bitcoin (BTC-USD) with new investments and products.

What's next? For the coming week, ~$75K is the main support level. Holding above these levels could open the door for further upside towards ~$85K. However, any negative news around geopolitics may pull the asset back to $70K levels, so investors should keep a close eye on upcoming updates.

Other crypto-linked stocks impacted by BTC's surge include MicroStrategy (MSTR), up ~10%; Robinhood (HOOD), up ~4%; RIOT Platforms (RIOT), up ~$6%; Coinbase (COIN), up ~3.70%; and Hut 8 (HUT), up ~4.53%.

data.title: Badger Meter anticipates 2026 organic revenue in line with 2025 as it agrees to acquire UDlive for $100M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Badger Meter (BMI) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "First quarter sales were down 9% year-over-year to $202 million," as "short-cycle order rates... were weaker than we anticipated, resulting in approximately $15 million to $20 million of lower revenue versus our internal expectations" (Chairman, President & CEO Kenneth Bockhorst).
  • "We previously communicated that our 2026 revenues would be weighted toward the back half of the year" and "our expectations for a solid second half have not changed," while "the softer start up to the year prompts us to anticipate full year 2026 organic revenue to be on balance with 2025" (Chairman, President & CEO Bockhorst).
  • "This project set represents between 2.6 million and 3.6 million connections over multiple years" and "we view the first half of 2026 as a short pause, not a break in our trajectory" (Executive Vice President of North America Municipal Utility Robert Wrocklage).
  • "We've implemented measured cost reduction actions including a 10% salary reduction for our executive officers for the next 6 months to maintain spending discipline and protect margin integrity" (Chairman, President & CEO Bockhorst).
  • "Total sales were $202 million," "gross margin was 41.7%," "operating earnings were approximately $35.2 million and operating margin was 17.4%," and "diluted earnings per share were $0.93" (VP, CFO & Treasurer Daniel Weltzien).

Outlook

  • "We continue to expect 2026 activity to be back-half weighted as awarded AMI projects advance into deployment" and "we expect sequential improvement in absolute quarterly revenue dollars as the year progresses" (Chairman, President & CEO Bockhorst).
  • "We expect second quarter 2026 organic revenue dollars to sequentially improve from the trough of Q1" but "expect them to be down year-over-year against the highest quarterly revenue figure in the company's history" (Chairman, President & CEO Bockhorst).
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s framing that "we expect this project pacing dynamic to extend throughout the first half of 2026," management in Q1 added that the Q1 short-cycle softness "is timing related and does not reflect a structural change" (Chairman, President & CEO Bockhorst).
  • Analyst estimates were provided in a separate JSON tag, but its fields did not match the required format described in the instructions (for example, quarter values were numeric rather than Q1–Q4), so no estimates comparison is included.

Financial Results

  • "Total sales were $202 million, representing a 9% decline year-over-year"; "utility water sales declined 10%"; and "sales for the flow instrumentation product line were down 4% year-over-year" (VP, CFO & Treasurer Weltzien).
  • "Lower metering product revenue was partially offset by increased BEACON SaaS, smart cover, water quality and network monitoring product revenues" and "collectively, beyond the meter product line growth was a bright spot in the quarter" (VP, CFO & Treasurer Weltzien).
  • "Selling, engineering and administrative expenses were $49.2 million" including "$1.2 million in transaction costs associated with the UDlive acquisition"; "the effective income tax rate was 24.8%"; and "we generated strong free cash flow in the quarter of about $30 million" (VP, CFO & Treasurer Weltzien).
  • "In the first quarter of 2026, we repurchased 256,000 shares for a total of $38 million and have $115 million left on our share repurchase authorization" (VP, CFO & Treasurer Weltzien).

Q&A

  • Nathan Jones, Stifel: Asked if the $15 million to $20 million short-cycle shortfall was weather-related or something else; Chairman, President & CEO Bockhorst answered, "there's really not one underlying thing" and called it "relatively normal," while Executive Vice President Wrocklage added, "we're not chalking it up to geographic weather" and said "this is absolutely timing-based."
  • Nathan Jones, Stifel: Pressed on PRASA timing and reliability; Chairman, President & CEO Bockhorst said, "the confidence is higher today than it was before" citing "the fact that we have a PO" and that "installation partners are lined up."
  • Jeffrey Reive, RBC Capital Markets: Asked the risk that late starts push into 2027 and whether the outlook is conservative; Chairman, President & CEO Bockhorst said, "our confidence level is better today than it was 90 days ago" as projects move toward deployment.
  • Quinn Fredrickson, Baird: Asked whether the flat organic outlook assumes a short-cycle recovery; Chairman, President & CEO Bockhorst said, "we do expect some upside... compared to the current quarter," while Executive Vice President Wrocklage cautioned, "realize what we're saying for the whole year is flat-ish."
  • Andrew Krill, Deutsche Bank: Asked about gross margin trajectory and then Section 232 tariffs; Executive Vice President Wrocklage said, "the 39% to 42% range, we still have confidence in" and on tariffs, "our tariff exposure... hasn't really changed," adding, "I don't think about tariffs differently than I have over the last couple of quarters."
  • Scott Graham, Seaport: Asked about incremental margins and whether PRASA hurts; Executive Vice President Wrocklage said PRASA gross margins are "not quite at the line average level" but "the SEA leverage... gets us back to an OP leverage that's in line with the rest of the business," and added, "our SCA dollars have been relatively flat."
  • William Grippin, Barclays: Asked about sewer monitoring market penetration and payback; Chairman, President & CEO Bockhorst said adoption is "less than 0.5% of the manhole covers in the U.S." and "there's almost an immediate payback on cleaning optimization."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing, repeatedly probing timing risk and visibility (for example, "what's the risk that some of the late to starts push into 2027?" and "does that like post downside risk to the guidance?").
  • Management tone in prepared remarks was slightly negative on the quarter but confidence-oriented on timing ("this first quarter outcome is timing related") and became more defensive in Q&A on specifics ("we're not going to comment on price project to project") (Chairman, President & CEO Bockhorst).
  • Versus the prior quarter’s more upbeat record-performance framing ("record sales, profitability and cash flow"), the current call used more variability language ("air pocket" and "uneven") while reiterating long-term positioning and project funnel visibility.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 emphasized "record sales, profitability and cash flow" and described project pacing as having "very little to do" with demand or budgets; Q1 emphasized a sales decline tied to both project pacing and short-cycle softness, with management saying it is "timing related" (Chairman, President & CEO Bockhorst; VP, CFO & Treasurer Weltzien).
  • Q4 highlighted PRASA as "approximately 1.6 million service connections" with shipments expected in 2026; Q1 added "the first significant purchase order" and said deployment partners are expected to begin activity "around midyear" (Executive Vice President Wrocklage).
  • Q1 introduced new capital allocation and cost actions not discussed in Q4 detail, including "a 10% salary reduction for our executive officers for the next 6 months" and a signed agreement to acquire UDlive.

Risks and Concerns

  • "Short-cycle order rates... were weaker than we anticipated" and management said there is "the least amount of visibility" in that part of the business (Chairman, President & CEO Bockhorst).
  • Management repeatedly highlighted timing variability in deployments, with Chairman, President & CEO Bockhorst noting that over a multi-year project "over a 3-month period, it's really not" predictable, and Executive Vice President Wrocklage citing PRASA-specific disruption risk: "it's to hurricanes."
  • On tariffs, Executive Vice President Wrocklage said exposure "hasn't really changed" but added it is "always subject to change" as of April 17.

Final Takeaway

Badger Meter management framed Q1’s sales decline as the intersection of AMI project pacing and a short-cycle “air pocket,” and said it now expects 2026 organic revenue (excluding UDlive) to be in line with 2025 while still “back-half weighted.” Executives pointed to awarded AMI projects totaling “between 2.6 million and 3.6 million connections,” disclosed a $100 million agreement to acquire U.K.-based UDlive (expected to be EPS accretive in year 1), and described near-term cost discipline actions—including a 10% executive salary reduction for six months—while reiterating that the Q1 weakness was “timing-based” rather than structural.
